title: Russia annexes Crimea
summary: On 18 March 2014 President Vladimir Putin, standing beside the newly-installed Crimean prime minister Sergey Aksyonov in St George's Hall of the Grand Kremlin Palace, signed a treaty declaring the Autonomous Republic of Crimea and the port city of Sevastopol admitted to the Russian Federation as its 84th and 85th subjects. The annexation followed a three-week operation in which unmarked Russian soldiers, the so-called 'little green men', seized the Crimean parliament and blockaded Ukrainian military bases; a hastily-organised referendum held under armed occupation on 16 March was declared by local authorities to have produced a 96.77% vote in favour of joining Russia. Kyiv, still in the middle of a change of government after the collapse of the Yanukovych presidency on 22 February, was unable to respond militarily. The seizure was the first forcible redrawing of a European border since 1945 and the first time a permanent member of the UN Security Council had absorbed the territory of a neighbouring state since the end of the Cold War. The UN General Assembly voted 100 to 11 on 27 March 2014 to reject its validity. The annexation triggered the first round of Western sanctions on Russia and set in motion the war in eastern Ukraine that would eventually escalate into the full-scale invasion of February 2022.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Viktor Yanukovych, whose flight from Kyiv on 22 February 2014 after months of Maidan protests set the stage for the Russian operation in Crimea.
image.credit: Administration of the President of Ukraine

sections.body:
Crimea's status had been contested since long before 2014. The peninsula, a mountainous spur into the Black Sea inhabited historically by Crimean Tatars, Ukrainians, Russians, Greeks and Karaites, had been part of the Russian Empire from Catherine the Great's annexation of 1783 until, in a decree of 19 February 1954 marking the three-hundredth anniversary of the Treaty of Pereyaslav, the Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev transferred the Crimean Oblast from the Russian to the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic. When the Soviet Union collapsed in December 1991, the transfer became an international border. A 1994 agreement, the Budapest Memorandum, in which the United States, the United Kingdom and Russia gave security assurances to Ukraine in exchange for its surrender of the Soviet nuclear arsenal on its soil, explicitly recognised Ukraine's existing frontiers, Crimea included. The Russian Black Sea Fleet, headquartered at Sevastopol since 1783, remained on the peninsula under a lease extended most recently by the Kharkiv Pact of April 2010.

The political trigger for the 2014 crisis was the Ukrainian Revolution of Dignity, or Euromaidan. In November 2013 President Viktor Yanukovych, under pressure from Moscow, suspended preparations for an Association Agreement with the European Union in favour of a Russian loan package. Peaceful protests in Kyiv's Independence Square grew into months of confrontation, culminating in the police shootings of 18-20 February 2014 in which more than one hundred protesters were killed. On the evening of 21 February Yanukovych signed a compromise agreement brokered by the French, German and Polish foreign ministers; that same night he fled the capital, and by 22 February the Verkhovna Rada, Ukraine's parliament, voted him out of office. Interim President Oleksandr Turchynov and Prime Minister Arseniy Yatsenyuk took charge in Kyiv.

Moscow denounced the events in Kyiv as an unconstitutional coup. On 22 and 23 February 2014, a meeting at Putin's Novo-Ogaryovo residence, later described by the president himself in a television documentary, took the decision to 'return Crimea to Russia'. Russian intelligence and special-forces units were already active on the peninsula. On 27 February a group of unmarked, heavily-armed soldiers seized the Supreme Council of the Autonomous Republic of Crimea in Simferopol, disarmed its security, and installed Sergey Aksyonov, the leader of the small Russian Unity party, as prime minister. Aksyonov's party had received four percent in the last regional election. Simferopol airport and the ferry terminal at Kerch were secured on the same night.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: Soldiers without insignia outside a government building in Simferopol on 2 March 2014. Moscow initially denied that these were Russian troops; Putin later confirmed they were.

sections.body:
Within days of the parliament seizure, thousands of soldiers in unmarked green uniforms, driving vehicles with painted-over identification, appeared at Ukrainian military installations across Crimea. They wore modern Russian equipment and carried standard-issue Russian weapons; their bearing and organisation were unmistakably professional. President Putin, at a 4 March press conference, insisted they were 'local self-defence forces' who had bought their uniforms 'in a shop'. He would drop the fiction in April 2014, publicly acknowledging that Russian regular forces had been deployed. Analysts identified units from the 45th Guards Independent Airborne Brigade, the 810th Naval Infantry Brigade based at Sevastopol, and elements of Russian military intelligence's Spetsnaz.

By 2 March the peninsula was effectively sealed. Ukrainian bases at Perevalne, Feodosia and Belbek were surrounded but not stormed; the small Ukrainian garrisons, without orders from a paralysed Kyiv, mostly held out under blockade for the next two weeks. On 6 March the Crimean Supreme Council, meeting under armed guard, voted to hold a referendum on joining Russia and moved the date forward twice, from 25 May to 30 March to 16 March. The referendum offered two options, both of which involved separation from Ukraine: joining Russia, or restoring the 1992 Crimean constitution which had claimed near-independent status. There was no option to preserve the existing status quo. International observers from established monitoring bodies such as the OSCE were absent; a scattering of European far-right and far-left politicians attended as invited observers. Aksyonov's authorities announced turnout of 83.1% and a 96.77% vote for accession to Russia. Independent Russian and Ukrainian estimates suggested actual turnout was considerably lower, particularly in Tatar and Ukrainian districts, where a boycott had been called.

On 17 March the Crimean parliament declared independence and requested admission to the Russian Federation. On 18 March, in a ceremony broadcast live from the Kremlin, Putin, Aksyonov, the speaker of the Crimean parliament Vladimir Konstantinov, and the mayor of Sevastopol Alexei Chaly signed the treaty of accession. In his speech Putin cited Kosovo's 2008 declaration of independence as precedent, invoked 'the Russian nation' as 'one of the largest, if not the largest, divided nations in the world', and characterised the reunification as the correction of a historic injustice. Russian troops now moved openly against the remaining Ukrainian bases; on 19 March the naval headquarters in Sevastopol was stormed. On 21 March the Russian Constitutional Court ruled the treaty legal and Putin signed the law of admission. The last Ukrainian units left Crimea at the end of March. On 27 March the UN General Assembly adopted Resolution 68/262 affirming Ukraine's territorial integrity and calling the referendum invalid, by 100 votes in favour, 11 against, and 58 abstentions.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The annexation immediately opened a wider war. From April 2014 armed groups in the Donetsk and Luhansk regions of eastern Ukraine, backed by Russian intelligence officers and, from the summer, by regular Russian units, seized government buildings and declared the Donetsk and Luhansk People's Republics. Kyiv launched an 'anti-terrorist operation'. In August 2014 a Ukrainian counter-offensive was halted by the intervention of Russian regular forces at Ilovaisk, and in early 2015 at Debaltseve. Two agreements, Minsk I in September 2014 and Minsk II in February 2015, froze the front lines but were never fully implemented by either side. By 2021 the Donbas war had killed some 14,000 people, most of them in the first year and a half.

International sanctions followed rapidly. On 17 March 2014 the European Union and the United States imposed the first travel bans and asset freezes on Russian and Crimean officials. After the shooting down of Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 over eastern Ukraine on 17 July 2014, killing all 298 people on board, the sanctions were widened to entire sectors of the Russian economy including energy, banking, and defence. Russia was expelled from the G8, which reverted to the G7. Sanctions were tightened repeatedly through 2014 and 2015. The Russian rouble lost roughly half its value against the dollar between mid-2014 and early 2015, and inflation spiked to 15% in early 2015, though the impact was cushioned by high oil prices and by the Central Bank of Russia's aggressive response.

On the peninsula itself Russian rule was consolidated through the introduction of Russian passports, Russian law, and the rouble. The Mejlis, the elected representative body of the Crimean Tatars, was banned as an 'extremist organisation' in April 2016 after its leaders, Mustafa Dzhemilev and Refat Chubarov, refused to accept the annexation. Prominent Tatar activists and Ukrainian citizens were arrested on terrorism charges; independent Russian and international human-rights organisations documented dozens of enforced disappearances in the years after 2014. A rail-and-road bridge across the Kerch Strait, at 19 kilometres the longest in Europe, was opened by Putin driving a Kamaz truck across it on 15 May 2018. It became a strategic Russian supply route and, from October 2022, a target of Ukrainian long-range strikes.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
For eight years the annexation of Crimea sat as an unresolved wound in European security. Neither the United States nor the European Union recognised the change of sovereignty; a majority of United Nations member states voted repeatedly to reaffirm Ukraine's territorial integrity. But the annexation was also not reversed. It set a template - deniable special-forces operations, staged referendums, the fait accompli backed by the credible threat of escalation - that Russian planners would reach for again in eastern Ukraine, and, after 2022, in Kherson and Zaporizhzhia.

Inside Russia the annexation produced a remarkable if temporary surge in Putin's domestic popularity, from around 65% in early 2014 to above 85% for most of the following year, and locked in a nationalist domestic narrative built around the recovery of 'historically Russian lands' and the confrontation with the West. The internal political space for opposition, already narrow, contracted further. The murder of the opposition politician and former deputy prime minister Boris Nemtsov on Bolshoy Moskvoretsky Bridge, within sight of the Kremlin, on 27 February 2015, coincided with the anniversary of the seizure of the Crimean parliament. Nemtsov had been preparing a report on Russian military involvement in Ukraine.

For Ukraine the annexation was the beginning of a national reforging. The Ukrainian armed forces, reduced to little more than 6,000 combat-ready troops in early 2014, were rebuilt with Western assistance into a much larger, more capable NATO-interoperable force by 2022. Public support for European Union and NATO membership rose steeply and durably. When Russian tanks crossed the border again on 24 February 2022, the country that met them was not the country of 2014. In the two decades after the collapse of the Soviet Union, Crimea's transfer of hands had been a footnote of the Khrushchev era; after 18 March 2014 it was the fault line running through European politics.

figures.name: Vladimir Putin
figures.role: President of Russia who ordered the operation and signed the treaty of accession on 18 March 2014

figures.name: Sergey Aksyonov
figures.role: Leader of the small Russian Unity party installed as prime minister of Crimea on 27 February 2014 after the armed seizure of the regional parliament

figures.name: Viktor Yanukovych
figures.role: Ukrainian president whose flight from Kyiv on the night of 21-22 February 2014 provided Moscow with its pretext for intervention
figures.credit: Presidential Administration of Ukraine, CC BY 4.0

figures.name: Petro Poroshenko
figures.role: Ukrainian oligarch and politician elected president on 25 May 2014, immediately after the annexation, and forced to fight the Donbas war that followed

figures.name: Mustafa Dzhemilev
figures.role: Veteran Crimean Tatar leader and former Soviet dissident who led the Tatar community's boycott of the March 2014 referendum and was subsequently banned from entering Crimea

images.caption: Armed personnel of the Russian Federation at the Ukrainian military base at Perevalne, Crimea, on 9 March 2014, a week before the disputed referendum.

sources.title: Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation
